literal: the body-and-ensouler relation; śarīra (body) + śarīrin (ensouler) + bhāva (relation, condition)
invariant_definition: The body-ensouler relation. In Viśiṣṭādvaita this is not a metaphor borrowed from devotion but the working ontology of the relation between *brahman* and world. Rāmānuja defines body functionally: what a conscious self supports, controls, and uses for its own end is its body. On that basis every *cit* and *acit* entity is the body of *brahman*, who is their inner self. The doctrine explains scriptural co-reference, divine immanence, ontological dependence, and world-reality at once: the world is not outside God, not numerically identical with God without remainder, and not illusory. It also governs the practical register of *śeṣatva*: the *jīva* is rightly lived as belonging to the Lord whose body it is. The term therefore presupposes *apṛthak-siddhi*, *antaryāmin*, and *śeṣa-śeṣi-bhāva*. 'Body-soul relation' is tolerable only if one avoids the Cartesian picture of two substances externally joined; the intended model is of inner control, dependence, and purposive subordination.

per_school.definition: This is the central metaphysical grammar of the school. The body is that which is supported, controlled, and used by a conscious self for its purposes; accordingly all selves and all matter form the body of Brahman, who is their *śarīrī* and *antaryāmin*. The doctrine yields the three familiar relations of controlled to controller, subordinate to principal, and supported to supporter. It is the ontological basis for reading Upaniṣadic identity passages without erasing plurality.

Advaita accepts the language of indwelling control at the level of *Īśvara* and empirical teaching, but not as the final structure of reality. The relation between controller and controlled remains within the domain of *nāma-rūpa* and *avidyā*; ultimate inquiry dissolves that relational grammar into non-dual identity. Hence *śarīra-śarīrī-bhāva* is pedagogically useful but not metaphysically ultimate.

Dvaita accepts that Viṣṇu indwells and controls all dependent realities, but does not collapse that into the Viśiṣṭādvaita claim that the universe is His body in a strict ontological sense. The point of indwelling is sovereignty, not qualified identity. The real difference between Lord, souls, and matter must remain intact if devotion and dependence are to be more than verbal.

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'body-soul relation.' In English that often imports a dualist picture of two independently intelligible substances later joined together. Rāmānuja's use is stricter and more structural: body means what is controlled, supported, and exists for the sake of the indwelling self. If that functional definition is dropped, the doctrine is reduced to pious metaphor and its metaphysical force disappears.
